articles.title: Pluralistic: 2024 (apart from the obvious) (11 May 2026)
articles.title_display: Doctorow on 2024, compromise politics, and Trump’s return
articles.summary: This is a political opinion essay, not a technical engineering writeup. Doctorow argues that Biden and Harris ran as continuity candidates, that Democratic “pizzaburger” compromise politics alienated voters, and that this helped clear the path for Trump’s return. He uses examples like Medicare drug-price negotiation, antitrust appointments, student debt, and court obstruction to support the argument, but there are no data, benchmarks, or implementation details. The piece is mainly useful as a political framing essay rather than a source of technical insight.

articles.title: Mythos Finds a Curl Vulnerability
articles.title_display: Mythos Finds One Real curl Vulnerability
articles.summary: Daniel Stenberg reports that Anthropic’s Mythos model was used to scan curl’s source tree and initially flagged five “confirmed security vulnerabilities,” but curl’s maintainers reduced that to one real vulnerability after manual review. The scan covered about 178K lines of code in curl’s src/ and lib/ directories and was run on a recent git commit. Four of the five findings were either false positives or just bugs, and the single confirmed issue is described as low severity and slated for disclosure with curl 8.21.0 in late June. Stenberg also notes that Mythos found roughly twenty bugs total, with very few false positives, but he argues the result does not show a dramatic leap over earlier AI security tools used on curl, which have already led to 200-300 fixes over the past 8-10 months. The post’s main practical takeaway is that AI code review is already materially improving security for large C projects, but this example suggests the current systems are better as strong supplemental analyzers than as magic vulnerability finders.

articles.title: Python Software Foundation: Strategic Planning at the PSF
articles.title_display: PSF outlines five-year strategic plan for Python
articles.summary: The Python Software Foundation has published high-level goals for a five-year strategic plan and is asking the community for feedback before a full draft arrives in early June. The plan focuses on financial sustainability, governance and transparency, diversity and inclusion, community empowerment, and partnerships, plus program priorities around PyPI and supply-chain security, core Python infrastructure, global community support, and developer growth. The board says the plan will inform budget and staffing decisions, be reviewed annually, and is meant to guide priorities rather than implementation details.

articles.title: Moving from lsp-mode in GNU Emacs to Eglot
articles.title_display: Switching GNU Emacs from lsp-mode to Eglot
articles.summary:
The author replaced a long-running GNU Emacs lsp-mode setup with Eglot and found the migration workable but more involved than expected. Most of the effort went into wiring up surrounding packages rather than Eglot itself: Corfu for completion, Consult for xref-style reference previews, and a mix of Flycheck/Flymake integration for diagnostics and quickfixes. The post also shows concrete configuration for disabling pylsp linters via `eglot-workspace-configuration`, using `.dir-locals.el` for project-scoped server settings, and auto-starting Eglot only for local files.

A recurring theme is that Eglot’s minimalism reduces UI clutter, but shifts more responsibility to Emacs configuration and package composition. The author notes that Eglot is less convenient than lsp-mode for server selection, temporary settings, and filtering noisy code actions, especially when using pylsp and gopls. This is a practical migration report for Emacs users who want a quieter LSP experience and are willing to manage more of the integration themselves.

articles.title: An AI coding agent, used to write code, needs to reduce your maintenance costs
articles.title_display: AI Coding Agents Must Cut Maintenance Costs
articles.summary: James Shore argues that AI coding agents only create lasting productivity gains if they reduce maintenance costs in proportion to the extra code they generate. Using a simple lifecycle model, he claims that faster output without lower maintenance just shifts the team into a long-term productivity decline, because every line of code compounds future bug fixes, cleanup, and upgrades. He illustrates this with spreadsheet-style scenarios: doubling output while also doubling maintenance causes gains to disappear quickly, and can leave teams worse off later; even doubling output with unchanged maintenance still eventually erodes productivity. The post is a conceptual critique rather than an empirical benchmark, but it is relevant to teams adopting coding agents in production. The practical takeaway is to evaluate AI tools not just on generation speed, but on whether they measurably lower long-term upkeep per unit of code.

articles.title: I'm going back to writing code by hand
articles.title_display: Why the Author Is Rewriting a Vibe-Coded Kubernetes TUI
articles.summary: The author archived a 234-commit, 7-month Go/Kubernetes TUI after concluding that AI-assisted coding was optimizing for features, not architecture. In the post, they point to concrete failure modes in the codebase: a 1,690-line `model.go`, a 500-line `Update()` with 110 switch branches, nine scattered `= nil` cleanup paths, positional `[]string` data that made sort logic depend on magic indices like `ra[3]`, and a goroutine-based update path that could race with UI rendering. The key takeaway is that Claude could reliably add isolated features such as live resource views, logs, and GPU fleet tables, but once the project grew, state ownership, keybinding conflicts, and cross-view coupling accumulated into a “god object.” The author’s practical response is to rewrite the tool in Rust and move architecture decisions into hand-written invariants and `CLAUDE.md` rules: typed structs instead of flattened arrays, separate view state, per-view keymaps, and message passing only through the main event loop. For engineers building AI-assisted software, the post is a useful cautionary example of how fast delivery can hide structural decay until the codebase becomes hard to reason about.

articles.title: PS3 Emulator Devs Politely Ask That People Stop Flooding It with AI PRs
articles.title_display: RPCS3 Devs Push Back on AI Pull Requests
articles.summary: RPCS3, the open-source PlayStation 3 emulator, publicly asked contributors to stop submitting undisclosed AI-generated code pull requests and said repeat offenders may be banned. The team’s complaint is not about AI in general, but about low-quality patches that arrive without understanding the codebase and create extra maintenance work for volunteers. The article also notes this is part of a broader pattern: Godot maintainers reported a similar flood of AI-generated PRs earlier this year. For open-source projects, the practical takeaway is that AI-assisted contributions still need real debugging, reviewable changes, and honest disclosure.

articles.title: Quoting New York Times Editors’ Note
articles.title_display: NYT Corrects AI-Generated Misquotation
articles.summary: The New York Times issued an editors’ note after a reporter used an AI-generated summary as if it were a real quote from Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre. The paper says the tool rendered a summary of his views as a quotation, and the reporter should have verified it before publication. The article was corrected to quote from an actual April speech, which did not include the “turncoats” remark. This is a cautionary example of why AI outputs used in reporting must be independently checked before they are treated as source material.

articles.title: Weekly Update 503
articles.title_display: Troy Hunt on the Instructure Extortion Deadline

articles.title: Running local models on an M4 with 24GB memory
articles.title_display: Qwen 3.5 9B on an M4 MacBook with 24GB
articles.summary:
This post reports a practical local-LLM setup on an M4 MacBook Pro with 24GB RAM using LM Studio, Pi, and OpenCode. The author found that several larger models — including Qwen 3.6 Q3, GPT-OSS 20B, and Devstral Small 24B — technically fit in memory but were unusable in practice, while Gemma 4B ran reliably but was weak at tool use. The best result came from Qwen 3.5-9B quantized to Q4_K_S, which delivered about 40 tokens per second, supported thinking mode, tool use, and a 128K context window.

The article includes concrete configuration snippets for LM Studio, Pi, and OpenCode, including model provider JSON, context-length settings, and prompt-template tweaks to enable thinking. It also gives two real workflow examples: using the model to interpret and fix Credo warnings in an Elixir project, and to reason through a dependency-conflict rebase. The first worked cleanly; the second exposed a failure mode where the model identified the conflict but then tried to continue the rebase without actually resolving the file, causing the process to hang.

Overall, this is a useful hands-on note for engineers exploring local coding assistants on consumer hardware, especially if they want a realistic picture of what smaller quantized models can and cannot do.

articles.title: Obsidian plugin was abused to deploy a remote access trojan
articles.title_display: Obsidian Plugin Abuse Delivers PHANTOMPULSE RAT
articles.summary: Security researchers describe a targeted campaign, REF6598, that abuses Obsidian shared vaults and community plugins to deliver a new RAT called PHANTOMPULSE. Attackers use LinkedIn and Telegram to social-engineer finance and crypto professionals into opening a malicious vault and enabling plugin synchronization, which activates compromised plugins such as Shell Commands and Hider. The infection chain uses PowerShell on Windows and AppleScript on macOS, then loads the payload in memory to reduce file-based detection. PHANTOMPULSE is notable for its command-and-control design: it queries the Ethereum blockchain for the latest transaction from a hard-coded wallet address and extracts the C2 IP address from that transaction data. The reported capabilities include keystroke capture, screenshots, file exfiltration, and arbitrary command execution. The article is operationally useful because it also lists concrete detections, including Obsidian spawning PowerShell or osascript, suspicious `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass` usage, unexpected blockchain-related network traffic, and changes under `.obsidian/plugins/`. For defenders, the key takeaway is that collaboration tools and note-taking apps can become delivery vectors when plugin trust and sync settings are abused.

articles.title: Local AI needs to be the norm
articles.title_display: Why Local AI Should Be the Default
articles.summary:
The article argues that many AI features should run on-device instead of being routed through OpenAI- or Anthropic-style APIs, because cloud AI adds fragility, privacy risk, vendor dependence, billing exposure, and network-related failure modes. As a concrete example, the author describes a native iOS client for Brutalist Report that generates article summaries locally using Apple’s Foundation Models APIs, avoiding server calls, user-content logging, and vendor accounts.

The post highlights two implementation patterns: first, chunking long article text into roughly 10k-character segments and summarizing each before combining them; second, using typed structured output with Swift `@Generable` models instead of prompting for JSON and hoping for valid formatting. The central engineering claim is that local models are well suited to tasks like summarization, classification, extraction, and normalization when the data already exists on the device.

This is a pragmatic advocacy piece rather than a benchmark-driven evaluation, but it offers a useful product and architecture argument for engineers building privacy-sensitive, local-first applications.

articles.title: Ask HN: What Are You Working On? (May 2026)
articles.title_display: Hacker News May 2026 Work-in-Progress Roundup
articles.summary:
This is a recurring Hacker News “What are you working on?” thread, not a single technical article. It aggregates hundreds of short project updates from developers across many areas: AI coding tools, databases, security, infra, games, CLI tools, and niche utilities. Most posts are brief self-descriptions, often with little implementation detail beyond a product pitch, personal motivation, or a GitHub link. A few replies contain useful technical nuggets, such as benchmark numbers, architecture notes, or deployment details, but the thread as a whole is mostly a broad community pulse check rather than a substantive engineering writeup.

For an engineering digest, its value is mainly discovery: it surfaces new open-source repositories, early-stage tools, and patterns people are exploring in production-adjacent work. The strongest parts are the comments that include concrete claims like performance figures, durability, sandboxing, or storage/latency tradeoffs; the weakest are the many announcements that read like launch posts or “building X” placeholders. Overall, this is useful for scanning project ideas and spotting emerging tooling trends, but it does not provide deep standalone technical insight.

articles.title: Out With the JS, In With the HTML
articles.title_display: Replacing JS Widget State with HTML Navigation
articles.summary: The post describes a small UI refactor for icon-size selection: instead of using a custom element and client-side JavaScript to mutate the DOM, the site now treats size changes as navigation to separate HTML pages such as `/sm`, `/md`, `/lg`, and `/xl`. The author says this removes duplicated client/server rendering logic and maintenance risk when markup changes. CSS view transitions were added so the page change feels smooth, including on mobile. The main takeaway is a pragmatic pattern for static sites: if an interaction can be expressed as navigation, you can often delete JavaScript and simplify the system.

articles.title: The linear algebra of bit twiddling
articles.title_display: Bit Twiddling Through GF(2) Linear Algebra
articles.summary: This post explains bitwise shifts, XOR, and AND as linear transformations over GF(2), using 8-bit examples and matrix representations. It shows why operations like `x ^ (x >> 2)` are invertible: the corresponding matrices are triangular with all 1s on the diagonal, so their determinants are 1 modulo 2. The article also maps bitwise AND to multiplication by a diagonal matrix and relates these ideas back to the Mersenne Twister tempering step. It is a clear mathematical explanation, but it is mostly an educational reframe rather than a new algorithm or benchmarked engineering result. Useful for developers who want a cleaner mental model for bit manipulation and invertibility in PRNG tempering code.

articles.title: Incident CVE-2024-Yikes
articles.title_display: Satirical CVE Report Skewers Package Manager Security
articles.summary: This is a satirical incident report about a fictional supply-chain attack that spreads from a compromised JavaScript maintainer account to a Rust compression library and then into a Python build tool. The post strings together dependency theft, malicious postinstall/build scripts, vendored Rust code, CI compromise, and noisy incident response, but it provides no real vulnerability analysis, exploit details, or concrete remediation evidence beyond generic security platitudes. Its value is mostly as a parody of package-manager security failures and organizational response theater. For engineers, it is a reminder that dependency trust, vendoring, and automated updates can create cascading risk across npm, Cargo, and Python ecosystems.

articles.title: Hardware Attestation as Monopoly Enabler
articles.title_display: GrapheneOS Warns Hardware Attestation Is Becoming a Monopoly Tool
articles.summary: GrapheneOS argues that Apple and Google are steadily expanding hardware-based attestation and pushing services to depend on it. The post points to Google’s Play Integrity API and Apple’s App Attest API as closely related mechanisms, and says Apple has already brought the model to the web through Privacy Pass while Google plans to follow. The technical concern is not a new exploit but a platform control pattern: more services begin requiring device-backed proofs that are tied to the dominant mobile ecosystems, which can reduce interoperability and weaken user choice. For engineers, this matters wherever authentication, anti-abuse, or fraud controls are being designed around device trust signals. The practical implication is that attestation-based gating can become an ecosystem lock-in mechanism, so teams should evaluate its privacy, accessibility, and portability costs before adopting it.

articles.title: Tracesofhumanity.org by Joanna Rutkowska
articles.title_display: Joanna Rutkowska Relaunches Personal Blog

articles.title: Reverse engineering Mersenne Twister with Linear Algebra
articles.title_display: Reverse Engineering Mersenne Twister with Linear Algebra
articles.summary: This post shows that the original Mersenne Twister’s 32-bit tempering step is invertible and can be modeled as a linear transformation over GF(2). The author builds the 32×32 matrix for the temper function in Python, verifies that matrix multiplication reproduces the same output bits as the bit-twiddling implementation, and then computes the inverse matrix to recover internal state. Since MT outputs 640 tempered state values before twisting, the article explains how observing outputs can reconstruct the full state and predict future values. It is a clear, mathematically grounded explanation of why MT is suitable as a PRNG but not a CSPRNG, and the matrix-based framing is useful for readers who want a conceptually simpler path to state recovery.

articles.title: GitHub Is Sinking
articles.title_display: GitHub Is Losing Developer Trust
articles.summary: This is an opinionated rant arguing that GitHub has become unreliable, bot-heavy, and increasingly hostile to developers after Microsoft’s acquisition. The post cites uptime complaints, a missing status page, and a cluster of recent “leaving GitHub” posts, but it provides no original measurements, incident analysis, or engineering evidence beyond links and assertions. It recommends considering alternatives such as Codeberg/Forgejo, Tangled, Gitea, GitLab, Bitbucket, or self-hosting a Git forge, and suggests keeping an exit plan rather than depending on GitHub alone. The practical takeaway is not a migration recipe but a general reminder that Git is portable and GitHub is optional.

articles.title: Louis Rossmann tells 3D printer maker Bambu Lab to 'Go (Bleep) yourself'
articles.title_display: Louis Rossmann tells 3D printer maker Bambu Lab to 'Go (Bleep) yourself'

articles.title: Think Linear Algebra (2023)
articles.title_display: Code-First Linear Algebra Book for Python Learners
articles.summary: Allen Downey’s Think Linear Algebra is a code-first, case-based introduction to linear algebra built around Python notebooks. The chapters use concrete examples such as PageRank on a museum-traffic network, GPS tracking, billiards, affine transforms, electrical circuits, stoichiometry, truss analysis, and least-squares regression with the General Social Survey. It emphasizes NumPy, SciPy, SymPy, and NetworkX, and is designed for interactive learning in Jupyter/Colab rather than formal theorem-first instruction. The main value is practical pedagogy: it connects vectors, matrices, projections, LU decomposition, null spaces, rank, and QR-based regression to working code and real systems, making it a useful free resource for engineers and data practitioners who want intuition plus implementation.

articles.title: Production engineering when trading billions of dollars a day [video]
articles.title_display: Jane Street on High-Stakes Production Engineering
articles.summary:
Jane Street’s Mark Doss explains how production engineering works when software directly affects trading P&L, with billions of dollars moving daily. The talk emphasizes that in this environment, every message matters, so alerting and incident response are business-critical rather than just operational hygiene. It argues that traditional SLO-based approaches can fall short for trading systems, where the cost of delayed detection or response can be immediate and measurable.

The talk is structured around the production environment, what that means for Jane Street’s engineering practice, and a realistic sample incident. It highlights defense in depth, tight cross-team communication, and the need to design monitoring around rare but high-impact failures rather than generic uptime targets. The video also includes a summary and Q&A, suggesting it is meant as a practical engineering overview rather than a product pitch.

For engineers building low-latency, high-availability, or financially critical systems, the main value is the operational framing: reliability work should be evaluated by business impact, not only service-level dashboards.
